entry.title: Re: sales document type 'LKJ'
entry.content:
Hello @harum 

Sales document type 'LKJ' depends on scope item 53X, if the document type is not available is likely that this scope item is not active. 

As this scope item is a self-service scope item, it's selectable in scoping, you can add it yourself through SAP Central Business Configuration. You can refer to Changes via Edit | SAP Help Portal

Best regards,
Luiza

entry.title: Summarize values for total amount of rows
entry.content:
Hi,

I have an issue in story reporting where I can't figure out how to count the sum of a column for a numeric point chart.

Currently, I have the data in a table chart. There are 9 061 rows and each row has a value for the column "planned working time (hours)".

I would like to sum the amount of planned working time (hours) - so the result should in this case be 68 948,3. However, when I use the below calculated measures, I get the result of 9 061 (the sum of all available rows, but not the sum of the values themselves).

Aggregation:

  • Operation: SUM
  • Measure: count
  • Aggregation dimensions: planned working time (hours)

Restricted measure

  • Measure: count
  • Dimensions: planned working time (hours)
  • Values: all

How do I proceed in order to get the sum of all available values for the column hours? Which in this case should be 68 948,3.

/Jessica

entry.title: Unable to Update Sales Order Item Pricing Condition via API_SALES_ORDER_SRV
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community,

I am trying to update the pricing of an existing sales order item in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition via OData API.

Scenario:

  • Sales Order Type: Standard Order
  • The sales order has already been created successfully.
  • Afterwards, I would like to change the price condition of an item using the API.

I am using the following PATCH request against API_SALES_ORDER_SRV:

https://myXXX.s4hana.cloud.sap/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_SALES_ORDER_SRV/A_SalesOrderItemPrElement(SalesOrder='278',SalesOrderItem='10',PricingProcedureStep='20',PricingProcedureCounter='1')

Request Body:

{
  "d": {
    "ConditionType": "ZPR0",
    "ConditionRateValue": "0",
    "ConditionCurrency": "EUR"
  }
}

The PATCH request is executed successfully and no error is returned. However, the condition value in the sales order is not updated.

Is it possible to update pricing conditions of an existing sales order via API_SALES_ORDER_SRV in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition?

Thanks in advance for your help!

Katharina

entry.title: Re: Transfer of Requirements for Return Orders
entry.content:
Hi All,

As per SAP Note 606282,  ATP always treats requirements (entries in tables VBBE and VBBS)  of return order or return delivery as planned issues. There is no option to treat these requirements as planned receipts. As a result, a return can never be viewed as a planned receipt in the ATP check.

entry.title: Re: Is LLM fine-tuning really dead?
entry.content:
That maps closely to how I think about it, with one addition @Marla-Ellinger.

Your triggers are mostly about reliability and cost: brittle prompts, expensive failures, and retry overhead. The factor that tips me most is slightly different. It's not the failure rate but rather where the failure lands.

A prompt that fails loudly and gets caught by schema validation is usually survivable. The cases that push me toward adapting the model are the silent failures: outputs that are perfectly schema-valid but quietly wrong. Validation can't catch those, so the error crosses the boundary into downstream systems.

When the cost of a wrong-but-well-formed answer is high, retries and guardrails stop being enough. That's usually my signal to adapt the model.

entry.title: SAP BTP - CI - Building Open-Source Custom Adapter EventSmartKafka from Zero with SAP ADK - Part II
entry.content:
“Most CPI developers configure adapters. Few understand what happens after deployment."

This independent research project exists to fill that gap: opening the adapter runtime box and helping the SAP Community understand what really happens inside SAP Cloud Integration after an iFlow is deployed. - Ricardo Viana, 03 June 2026

Part II — What Happens After iFlow Deployment: Consumer Runtime, Polling and Offset Commit

Most SAP Cloud Integration developers use adapters every day.

Very few understand what happens inside one after an iFlow is deployed.

This article opens that runtime box.

In Part I, I introduced the motivation and architecture behind EventSmartKafka, an open-source custom Kafka adapter for SAP Integration Suite / Cloud Integration.

Part II does not focus on Maven setup, project generation, OSGi packaging, or how to create the adapter project from zero. That belongs to Part III.

This article focuses on the runtime behavior behind the CPI channel:

  • how metadata.xml defines the adapter UI
  • how UI fields become Java Endpoint properties
  •  how the Consumer is created after iFlow deployment
  • how doStart() initializes the Kafka client
  •  how doPoll() reads Kafka records
  • how payload validation and Avro conversion happen before the CPI pipeline
  • how poison messages are handled and why offset commit must happen only after successful processing.

Kafka connectivity is only one part of the story.

The real adapter problem is controlling runtime behavior without silent payload loss, uncontrolled retry loops, hidden offset commits, or generic monitoring errors.

What to Expect: The 7-Part Deep-Dive Journey

To fully unpack the engineering behind an enterprise-grade SAP adapter, this initiative is structured into seven comprehensive technical chapters:

Part

Title

Focus

I 

The Journey & Architecture Overview

Project vision, metadata configuration, visual logging frameworks, and open-source delivery roadmap. 

II ★

Core Consumer Lifecycles & Polling Loops

Java runtime deep dive: how the Apache Camel polling consumer lifecycle maps to Kafka cluster subscription models. (This article)

III

The Multi-Profile Security Engine

Technical breakdown of SASL, mTLS, and OAuth 2.0 dynamic token integration using the SAP Secure Store.

IV

Multi-Threaded Parallel Consumers & Scale

Engineering for high-throughput. Parallel threading pools inside Cloud Integration worker nodes without JVM memory leaks.

V

Advanced Payload Conversion & Schema Registry Governance

Avro-to-JSON/XML serialization layer, caching strategies, and memory protection mechanisms.

VI

Designing the Producer (Receiver) Integration Role

Outbound flow: partition keys, acknowledgment levels (acks), and asynchronous message dispatching.

VII

Visual Operations, Error Interception & Production Sign-Off

Custom error UI rendering, transaction boundary management, and production go-live best practices.

Status: Community Demo / Experimental Technical Preview

Before going deeper, I want to make the positioning explicit.

EventSmartKafka is being released as a Community Demo / Experimental Technical Preview.

It is:

  • Not SAP-certified;
  • Not enterprise stress-tested;
  • Not SLA-backed;
  • Not a commercial product;
  • Not a replacement for vendor-supported Kafka adapters.

The objective is educational, maybe with future contribution can be something interessting for all.

The goal is to help the SAP Community understand what happens inside a custom adapter runtime after an iFlow is deployed.

The first public baseline focuses on the Consumer side — implemented as a CPI Sender Adapter:

  • Kafka event ingestion
  • raw passthrough
  • Avro to JSON conversion
  • Avro to XML conversion
  • Schema Registry integration
  • Confluent Magic Wire Format
  • Fixed Schema ID resolution
  • SASL / PLAIN
  • SCRAM-SHA-256
  • SCRAM-SHA-512
  • TLS connectivity
  • controlled runtime safety limits
  • compact CPI monitoring errors
  • poison message handling
  • offset commit control.

Producer-side validation, Protobuf, OAuth 2.0, and full mTLS validation remain roadmap items for later parts.

Production usage requires independent validation by the adopting organization.

Package Overview in SAP Integration Suite

Figure 1 — EventSmartKafka package inside SAP Integration Suite, positioned as a Community Demo / Experimental Technical Preview.

SAP ADK - The open box for SAP SCN Community

SAP adapters are often treated as black boxes.

Developers configure them, deploy iFlows, and wait for messages to arrive — but very few see what happens inside the adapter runtime.

With the SAP Adapter Development Kit, that box can be opened.

A custom adapter exposes the real internal layers behind a CPI channel: metadata.xml, Component, Endpoint, Consumer lifecycle, polling logic, payload handling, error strategy, and offset control.

The objective of this article is not only to show EventSmartKafka as a Kafka adapter.

The objective is to open the box and make the adapter runtime understandable for the SAP Community.

Figure 2 — SAP ADK opens the adapter black box: from design-time metadata to runtime Consumer behavior.

SDIA and ODCP — The Invisible Domain Layer

EventSmartKafka is also an implementation example of SDIA — Semantic Domain Integration Architecture — and ODCP — Orchestration Domain-Centric Pattern for SAP CPI.

SDIA does not replace tools, platforms, or existing governance models. It changes the operating model. The same artifacts remain: APIM, CPI, Kafka topics, metadata, security objects, iFlows, scripts, and backend services. What changes is the discipline used to organize and operate them.

When applied correctly, SDIA creates an invisible Domain Layer above the integration stack. This layer gives business meaning, ownership, boundaries, and operational control to technical artifacts across APIM, CPI, Kafka, and backend systems.

SDIA is a free and open architecture released under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International — CC BY 4.0.

That is the principle behind SDIA: technology executes, but the domain governs.

Zenodo: Semantic Domain Integration Architecture (SDIA): A Vendor‑Agnostic Semantic Governance Model for Enterprise Integration - v3.0 - FINAL

Zenodo: Orchestration Domain-Centric Pattern (ODCP): A Vendor-Agnostic Domain-Semantic Orchestration Topology for Enterprise Integration Platforms - v3.0 - FINAL

Figure 13 — SDIA defines the invisible governance layer above technical artifacts, keeping domain ownership consistent across adapters, iFlows, metadata, Kafka topics, security, and runtime behavior.

Why the Metadata Comes First

In SAP Cloud Integration custom adapter development, the UI is not a secondary detail.

The metadata.xml file is the contract between the design-time UI and the Java runtime.

It defines:

  • adapter variants
  • sender/receiver direction
  • tabs
  • attribute groups
  • field labels
  • dropdown values
  • default values
  • visibility conditions
  • runtime parameter names

If the metadata exposes a parameter that the Java endpoint does not understand, the adapter archive may still deploy, but the iFlow can fail when the endpoint is created.

Figure 3 — From the medata.xml to adapter UI visual

This is one of the most common custom adapter problems:

  • ESA deploys successfully
  • Channel appears in the iFlow
  • iFlow deployment fail
  • Endpoint cannot be created
  • Unknown parameter or missing JavaBean property

That is why the metadata and the endpoint class must evolve together.

If the UI exposes this:

<AttributeReference>
    <ReferenceName>schemaRegistryHostAddress</ReferenceName>
</AttributeReference>

Then the endpoint.class must expose this:

@UriParam
private String schemaRegistryHostAddress;

public String getSchemaRegistryHostAddress() {
    return schemaRegistryHostAddress;
}

public void setSchemaRegistryHostAddress(String schemaRegistryHostAddress) {
    this.schemaRegistryHostAddress = schemaRegistryHostAddress;
}

The name must match.The type must be compatible.The getter and setter must follow JavaBean conventions.

No mismatch.No guesswork.No hidden alias unless intentionally supported.

For EventSmartKafka, the current Consumer UI is organized into five major tabs:

  • Connection
  • Processing
  • Conversion
  • Conversion Advanced
  • Advanced

Each tab exists for a reason.

The objective is not to expose hundreds of parameters. The objective is to expose the right parameters at the right level, with clear separation between connection, polling, conversion, and runtime behavior.

The metadata.xml Opened in the IDE

Figure 4 — Adapter metadata defines the design-time UI exposed inside the SAP Cloud Integration channel.

Endpoint Java Properties

Figure 5 — Endpoint Java properties must match the runtime parameter names defined in metadata.xml.

Result:

Figure 6 — Adapter UI of Authentication Methods that comes from the metadata.xml, read by java SdiaKafkaEndpoint.class after the compilation into .esa ( adapter form) 

The Component Class

The Component class is the adapter entry point.

Its job is not to process Kafka records.

Its job is to connect the adapter scheme with the Camel runtime and create endpoint instances.

Conceptually, it looks like this:

public class SdiaKafkaComponent extends DefaultComponent {

    @Override
    protected Endpoint createEndpoint(
            String uri,
            String remaining,
            Map<String, Object> parameters) throws Exception {

        SdiaKafkaEndpoint endpoint = new SdiaKafkaEndpoint(uri, this);
        setProperties(endpoint, parameters);
        return endpoint;
    }
}

The important line is this:

setProperties(endpoint, parameters);

This is where Camel maps URI/metadata parameters into Java endpoint properties.

If the metadata sends a parameter that the endpoint does not expose, the endpoint creation may fail.

That is why the Component is simple, but critical.

It is the bridge between:

SAP Cloud Integration channel configuration
           ↓
Camel parameter map
           ↓
Endpoint JavaBean properties
           ↓
Consumer runtime behavior

A good Component class should stay boring.It should create the endpoint, apply parameters, and avoid business logic.

  • Kafka logic does not belong here.
  • Avro logic does not belong here.
  • Schema Registry logic does not belong here.

Component Class

Figure 7 — The Component class creates the Endpoint and connects the adapter scheme with Apache Camel.

The Endpoint Class

The Endpoint class is the runtime configuration contract.

It receives, stores, validates, normalizes, and exposes the adapter configuration that comes from the SAP Cloud Integration channel UI.

For EventSmartKafka, the endpoint is responsible for configuration areas such as:

  • Kafka bootstrap servers
  • topic pattern
  • partitions
  • consumer group ID
  • authentication mode
  • SASL mechanism
  • TLS flag
  • credential aliases
  • Cloud Connector Location ID
  • conversion format
  • Schema Registry host
  • Schema Registry credential
  • Schema ID resolution mode
  • payload safety limits
  • poll timeout
  • fetch settings
  • retry settings
  • runtime timing parameters

The Endpoint should not poll Kafka.

The Endpoint should not decode Avro.

The Endpoint should not commit offsets.

The Endpoint should provide the Consumer with a clean, validated configuration model.

A production-minded endpoint should follow three rules:

  • Fail fast on invalid configuration
  • Normalize values once
  • Expose explicit getters for runtime classes

For example, if the user selects PROTOBUF while Protobuf is not implemented yet, the adapter must not silently pass the record as raw bytes.

Avro Convertor possibilities:

It must fail clearly.

A silent fallback is worse than an exception.

This is especially important in event-driven architecture because silent failures can corrupt downstream business behavior while the monitor still looks green.

Endpoint Configuration Blocks

Figure 7 — SdkaKafkaEndpoint.class

Figure 8 — The Endpoint class receives and validates channel configuration before the Consumer runtime starts.

The Consumer Class: Where Kafka Meets CPI

The Consumer is the heart of the first public baseline.

In EventSmartKafka, the first release is Consumer-first.

This means the main validated flow is:

Kafka topic
     ↓
EventSmartKafka Consumer Adapter
     ↓
SAP Cloud Integration iFlow
     ↓
Downstream processing

The Consumer maps the Apache Camel ScheduledPollConsumer lifecycle to the Kafka KafkaConsumer polling model.

At runtime, the Consumer must:

  • initialize configuration
  • resolve credentials
  • connect to Kafka
  • subscribe to topics or assign partitions
  • poll records
  • optionally convert payloads
  • create a Camel Exchange
  • send the Exchange to the CPI pipeline
  • commit the Kafka offset only after successful processing
  • handle failures without silent data loss

The simplified runtime flow is:

KafkaConsumer.poll()
        ↓
ConsumerRecord<String, byte[]>
        ↓
raw passthrough or Avro conversion
        ↓
Camel Exchange
        ↓
Integration Flow pipeline
        ↓
successful processing
        ↓
commit offset

Reading from Kafka is not the hard part.

The hard part is committing the correct offset at the correct time.

 Consumer Class

Figure 9 — The Consumer maps the Camel polling lifecycle to Kafka record consumption.

Kafka to CPI Runtime Flow

Figure 10 — Consumer-first runtime flow from Kafka polling to SAP Cloud Integration message processing.

Why Offset Handling Is the Real Problem

A Kafka consumer can read a record in a few lines of code.

That is not adapter engineering.

Adapter engineering starts when something fails.

The key question is:

When is it safe to commit the offset?

For EventSmartKafka, the principle is simple:

Do not commit the offset until the message is successfully delivered to the CPI pipeline.

For raw passthrough, this means:

  • Read record
  • Create Exchange
  • Send payload to CPI pipeline
  • Pipeline succeeds
  • Commit offset

For Avro conversion, this means:

  1. Read record
  2. Validate payload
  3. Resolve Schema ID
  4. Fetch schema
  5. Decode Avro
  6. Create JSON or XML
  7. Send payload to CPI pipeline
  8. Pipeline succeeds
  9. Commit offset

If Avro conversion fails, the offset must not be committed.

Otherwise, the consumer group moves forward and the failed record is lost from the integration process.

That is exactly what a serious Kafka adapter must avoid.

The current behavior model is:

Scenario Offset Commit

Raw payload delivered successfullyYes
Avro converted and delivered successfullyYes
Missing Confluent Magic ByteNo
Invalid Schema IDNo
Schema Registry errorNo
Raw payload above hard limitNo
Conversion payload above hard limitNo
CPI pipeline failure with Stop on ErrorNo
CPI pipeline failure with Skip Failed MessageYes

This is why offset management belongs in the adapter layer.

If every iFlow must solve this independently, governance disappears.

Figure 11 — Offset commit must happen only after successful conversion and pipeline delivery.

Poison Pills and Silent Commits: The Two Failure Modes

A poison pill is a bad Kafka record that can be polled but cannot be processed. The consumer remains alive, but if the offset is not committed and no controlled strategy is applied, the same bad record is read again and again, blocking the next valid offsets.

A silent commit is the opposite problem. It happens when the adapter commits the offset even though the record failed. The consumer moves forward, the monitor may look green, but the failed message is lost.

EventSmartKafka is designed to make this behavior explicit: failed conversions are visible in CPI monitoring, poison messages are controlled by the selected error strategy, and offsets are committed only when processing is considered successful.

Figure 12 — Poison pills and silent commits are opposite failure modes: one blocks progress, the other hides data loss. A serious adapter must make both behaviors visible and controlled.

After iFlow Deployment: Runtime Lifecycle ASCII

[ SAP Integration Suite (CPI) ] Deploys iFlow
                             |
                             v
               Custom Adapter ESA is loaded
                             |
                             v
               [ Apache Camel ] Creates Component
                             |
                             v
           SdiaKafkaComponent.createEndpoint(...)
                             |
                             v
                 metadata.xml parameters
                     are mapped into
                SdiaKafkaEndpoint setters
                             |
                             v
           SdiaKafkaEndpoint.createConsumer(...)
                             |
                             v
               SdiaKafkaConsumer is created
                             |
                             v
                         doStart()
             ├── Validate Endpoint Configuration
             ├── Resolve CPI Secure Store Credentials
             ├── Build Native Kafka Client Properties
             ├── Instantiate KafkaConsumer Client
             └── Subscribe to Topic / Assign Partitions
                             |
                             v
             ScheduledPollConsumer Loop Starts
                             |
                             v
                         doPoll()
             ├── KafkaConsumer.poll() -> ConsumerRecord
             ├── Validate Payload Size Limits (Max 20MB)
             ├── Optional Conversion (AVRO/Protobuf -> JSON)
             ├── Create Camel Exchange & Inject Headers
             └── Dispatch Exchange to CPI Pipeline
                             |
                             v
                    CPI iFlow Processing
                             |
                +------------+------------+
                |                         |
         [ YES ]|                         |[ NO ]
                v                         v
          commitSync()             Evaluate Error Strategy
                |                   ├── 🔁 Retry Attempt
                v                   ├── ⏭️ Skip Message
         Next Poll Cycle            └── 🚨 Stop Pipeline

Runtime Class Responsibility

  • SdiaKafkaComponent: Creates the Endpoint and maps Camel parameters.
  • SdiaKafkaEndpoint: Stores and validates channel configuration.
  • SdiaKafkaConsumer: Starts Kafka connection, polls records, creates exchanges, controls offset commit.
  • SdiaKafkaAvroConverter: Converts Avro binary payloads into JSON/XML when conversion is enabled.
  • SdiaSchemaRegistryClient: Fetches and caches schemas by schema ID.

Compact Error Rendering

A custom adapter should not hide runtime failures behind generic Java stack traces.

For EventSmartKafka, conversion and payload validation errors are rendered in a compact CPI monitoring format.

The objective is to show the relevant runtime context directly in the monitor:

  • topic
  • partition
  • offset
  • bootstrap host
  •  schema mode
  • schema ID when available
  • error code
  • root cause

The current demo baseline includes visible error rendering for scenarios such as:

  • Avro payload without Confluent Magic Byte
  • invalid or unexpected Schema ID
  • Schema Registry lookup failure
  • payload above the configured conversion limit
  • payload above the raw passthrough limit

This matters because poison messages must be visible, traceable, and controlled.

A failed conversion should not silently commit the offset and disappear from the integration flow.

Figure 13 — Compact CPI error for Avro payload without valid Confluent Magic Byte.

Figure 14 — Compact CPI error for payload above the configured conversion limit.

What Has Been Tested So Far

The current Consumer demo baseline was validated in controlled non-production environments using trial, free-tier, or limited cloud resources.

Validated scope includes custom ESA deployment, iFlow channel creation, Kafka consumer startup, SASL/PLAIN over TLS, SCRAM-SHA-256/512, raw passthrough, Avro to JSON/XML, Schema Registry integration, Confluent Magic Wire, Fixed Schema ID, compact CPI monitor errors, conversion failure without offset commit, payload limits, and schema response limits.

This is not enterprise stress testing. It would be unrealistic to claim that an independent open-source project, developed and tested by one person, has validated every broker, security profile, network condition, payload size, partition strategy, retry behavior, and failure scenario.

All broker validations used trial, free-tier, or limited environments. These tests prove functional behavior under controlled conditions, not enterprise-scale certification.

Validated connectivity:
Confluent Cloud, Redpanda, Aiven.

Explored / partially tested:
IBM Event Streams.

What Is Explicitly Not in the First ESA

The first public ESA is Consumer-only.

It does not include validated Producer / Receiver role, Protobuf conversion, OAuth 2.0 production validation, full mTLS production validation, enterprise-scale performance certification, long-running endurance testing, vendor SLA, or SAP certification.

This is intentional. A community release should not mix validated functionality with roadmap items.

Figure 15 — EventSmartKafka - Custom Kafka.esa

What Part III Will Cover

Part III will explain how to create the custom adapter project from zero using SAP ADK and will provide the first downloadable .esa package on GitHub as a Community Demo / Experimental Technical Preview.

Users will be able to deploy the ESA in SAP Integration Suite, create a test iFlow, configure the EventSmartKafka Sender Adapter, and validate the Consumer runtime behavior.

The release will include basic documentation, configuration guidance, known limitations, and controlled test instructions.

The complete Java source code will be opened progressively in the next parts of the series.

Figure 16 — From blog to code to integration: Part III will provide the first downloadable ESA package on GitHub for controlled deployment and testing in SAP Cloud Integration.

Conclusion

Part I explained why EventSmartKafka exists.

Part II opened the runtime box and showed what happens after an iFlow is deployed: metadata.xml becomes UI, UI parameters become Endpoint properties, the Consumer starts, doStart() prepares the Kafka client, doPoll() reads records, and offset commit is controlled after processing.

The main lesson is simple: reading from Kafka is easy. Safe runtime behavior is the real adapter engineering challenge.

That means controlling poison pills, avoiding silent commits, exposing compact CPI errors, and committing offsets only when processing is considered successful.

EventSmartKafka remains a Community Demo / Experimental Technical Preview: not SAP-certified, not SLA-backed, and not a replacement for vendor-supported adapters.

Part III will explain how to create the SAP ADK adapter project from zero and will provide the first downloadable ESA package on GitHub for controlled testing.

The journey continues in Part III.

Part I - SAP BTP - CI - How I Built an Open-Source Enterprise Custom Kafka Adapter from Scratch - Part I

Ricardo Viana
Independent Solo Researcher

Creator of DEIP · SDIA · GDCR · DDCR · ODCP · EDCP · DDCP

#SAPIntegrationSuite #SAPCloudIntegration #Kafka #SAPADK #OpenSource #EventDrivenArchitecture #ApacheKafka #Avro #SchemaRegistry #CloudIntegration #SAPCommunity

entry.title: SAP Enable Now - Translation function stopped working
entry.content:
Hi Team,

Where can I find any technical information regarding the Translation function? I'm using Producer, licenced with the Machine Translation capability. We have been developing and translating for 3 months, but today, it stopped. Once I select the Machine Translation option in Tools, the system generates and reads objects as if it is busy with translating the course. Then it finishes, with no obvious error, but there is no translated course to be found. We have translated for all courses and they are all set to the same standards.
- where can I see an error log and how would I read it?
- Could it be a space issue on the SEN server?

entry.title: BDC <-> S4/HANA Integration
entry.content:
Hello Community,

Has anyone achieved something similar along the lines of sending mass bookings from Datasphere or SAC into S/4HANA system before?

I am tasked with building such a system integration.

What would be the preferred / SAP standard architecture to achieve the above?

Options I am currently taking into consideration:

  • Trigger odata API Call as custom data action from SAC from a seamless planning model
  • Trigger odata API Call as scheduled job from Datasphere
  • Move data via replication flow from Datasphere to S4/HANA custom intermediate table, then find out how to create bookings based on a custom table in S4 

The requirements are:

  • Millions of monthly bookings generated within BDC need to be sent in batch.
  • The operation needs transaction controls if it fails (e.g. run ACID-compliant / log successfully posted transactions / re-start where you left off on failure of one system).

So far, I found the following links:

  • API Reference | Journal Entry - Post (Asynchronous) | SAP Business Accelerator Hub
  • Journal Entry - Post (Asynchronous) | SAP Help Portal

However, using odata, I am wondering if it scales or if e.g. some kind of replication flow allows to write data en masse back into S4 custom table to then create bookings based on that with a scheduled job if that would be preferred architecturally and is possible.

I first need to showcase it is possible to book it in an S/4HANA system now incl. tests (smoke test, scalability with generated data, E2E). How would you achieve this quickly & reliably?

Thank you for any hints,

entry.title: NO_Broker Invoice Balance Due Not Visible After Payment in Supplier/Customer Line Items Report
entry.content:
Dear SAP OEM Support Team,

We are facing an issue in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud related to the visibility of the outstanding invoice balance after payments are posted against supplier/customer invoices.

Issue Details:

  • Supplier and Customer invoices are posted successfully.
  • Payments are subsequently posted against the invoices (including partial payment scenarios).
  • In the Supplier/Customer Line Items report, the invoice documents and payment documents are visible.
  • However, the remaining Invoice Balance Due is not displayed after the payment is applied to the invoice.

Expected Behavior:
After a payment is posted against an invoice, the report should display the updated outstanding balance (remaining amount due) for the invoice, allowing users to easily identify:

  • Original invoice amount
  • Amount paid
  • Remaining balance due/open amount

Actual Behavior:
The report shows the invoice and related payment transactions,

entry.title: No More Blank Display Names: How to Update User Attributes with Identity Provisioning
entry.content:
Provisioning users from source to target systems is usually straightforward: you run the job, sync the data, and everything works. At some point, however, new requirements come up — such as the need to update the attributes of existing users.

Consider this: Your target system now requires human‑readable display names for all users. This isn’t just a cosmetic change but a real scenario. In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, business partners representing business (login) users need meaningful identifiers. Without display names, SAP Task Center users are left identifying tasks by UUIDs, which are neither intuitive nor easy to remember.

So how do you handle that?

Your Three Options

When facing this requirement, you have the following options:

  • Manual Entry - Enter the attribute manually. This is technically possible but isn’t scalable for hundreds or thousands of users.
  • Custom Development - Build your own solution using the Identity Directory API. This gives you full control but requires development resources and maintenance.
  • Identity Provisioning Service - Modify your transformation mappings and run provisioning jobs. This is the approach we’ll guide you through in this blog.

The Practical Example

Let’s update the displayName attribute for a user named Michael Adams in the Identity Directory of your SAP Cloud Identity tenant. For simplicity, the system is configured to sync within the same Identity Directory, meaning it acts as both the source and the target.

Currently, Michael’s account contains basic attributes: first name, last name, login name, email, and department, but the display name is empty.

 

 

To populate the Display Name with a value:

  1. Login to SAP Cloud Identity Services admin console and navigate to Identity Provisioning.
  2. Select your target Identity Directory system, open the Transformations tab and choose Edit.
  3. Apply one of the following mapping changes:

Populate Existing Attribute in the Display Name

To populate an existing attribute (for example, userName) to the display name, replace the current displayName mapping:

 

{
               "sourcePath": "$.displayName",
               "optional": true,
               "targetPath": "$.displayName"
 },

With:

{
                "sourcePath": "$.userName",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$.displayName"
   },

 

After saving your changes and running the job, the userName (login name: MAdams) is populated in the Display Name.

 

 

Concatenate First and Last Names

To concatenate the first and last names into the displayName, replace the current mapping with one that uses the concatString function:

 

{
   "sourcePath":"$.name.givenName",
   "targetPath":"$.displayName",
   "functions":[
      {
         "function":"concatString",
         "suffix":"$.name.familyName"
      }
   ]
},

 

After saving your changes and running the job, the system combines the first and last names into the Display Name.

 

 

Use Attributes from Extended SCIM Schema

When using attributes outside the default core SCIM schema - for example, combining the last name with a department value (Finance/Accounting), where department belongs to the enterprise extension schema, you must explicitly reference the schema in your mapping:

 

{
   "sourcePath":"$.name.familyName",
   "targetPath":"$.displayName",
   "functions":[
      {
         "function":"concatString",
         "suffix":"$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:enterprise:2.0:User']['department']"
      }
   ]
},

 

After saving your changes and running the job, the system concatenates the last name (Adams) and department (Finance/Accounting) into the Display Name.

 

 

Concatenate First Name, Last Name, and Username

To combine three attributes, for example: first name, last name, and username into the displayName, replace the current mapping with one that uses the replaceParameters function:

 

{
                "constant": "<firstName> <lastName> (<userName>)",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$.displayName",
                "functions": [
                    {
                        "function": "replaceParameters",
                        "placeholders": {
                            "firstName": "$.name.givenName",
                            "lastName": "$.name.familyName",
                            "userName": "$.userName"
                        }
                    }
                ]
            },

 

After saving your changes and running the job, the Display Name will include all three attributes.

 

 

As you can see, there are multiple ways to update user attributes. Once you understand the transformation logic, you can adapt it to fit your business scenarios. The result: less manual effort, greater efficiency, and no need for custom code.

entry.title: Re: Is there any size limit for individual files included...
entry.content:
Hi @peter_schwartz3 :  There isn't any other setting that's explicitly maintained for controlling the size of individual files included in a SCORM zip file. The entire content zip file is considered as a one single file & that limit is based on the below configuration.

System Administration > Configuration > System Configuration > CONTENT IMPORT >  maximumFileSize. 

I believe that is the confirmation you are looking for.

entry.title: Item Text, Note to payee and uploading customer invoices
entry.content:
Hi SAP experts!

My client wants to collect SEPA direct debits from customers. The receivables are to be posted using the ‘Upload customer open items’ app. The payment reference/note to payee should include the document date (FPAYP-BLDAT) and the item text (FPAYP-SGTXT) so that the customer knows what the money is being collected for.

I have learnt that the item text is only transferred to the payment reference if it is entered in the document preceded by an asterisk ‘*’, e.g. *Service Fee. However, if I add an asterisk at the start in the Excel file used to import the receivables, the system removes this asterisk during posting. When entering an outgoing invoice manually, the asterisk remains, but it is removed during automatic import.

How can I resolve this issue?

Thank you and best regards,

Rado

entry.title: Unlocking Business Insights with AI-Assisted Context Analyzer in SAP Signavio Process Intelligence
entry.content:
Unlocking Business Insights with AI-Assisted Context Analyzer in SAP Signavio Process Intelligence

 

From Unstructured Text to Valuable Insight 

Organizations today generate large volumes of unstructured business text, including customer, employee, and supplier feedback, as well as operational notes. However, much of this data remains underused. 

Typical sources include: 

  • Customer surveys, support tickets, and emails 
  • Sales order notes, invoice comments, and dispute descriptions 
  • Field logs, warehouse notes, and production comments 

Individually, these data points provide useful signals. However, without process context, they are difficult to translate into action. 

AI-assisted context analyzer in SAP Signavio Process Intelligence connects these data sources to process execution. 

It enables organizations to: 

  • Understand what customers or employees are saying 
  • Link feedback directly to process steps 
  • Identify root causes of inefficiencies and experience gaps 

With capabilities such as sentiment analysis and text-to-event matching, organizations can transform unstructured text into actionable, process-driven insights. 

 

Why AI-Assisted Context Analyzer Matters 

AI-assisted context analyzer enables a new layer of analysis where business text becomes part of process intelligence instead of being reviewed in isolation. 

Key Value 

  • Identify root causes hidden in text data 
  • Correlate process deviations with real explanations 
  • Reduce manual investigation effort 
  • Improve both customer experience and operational performance 

 

Core Capabilities

1) Sentiment Analysis
Automatically classifies free text into: positive Neutral, or negative to help identify where customer or employee experience requires attention. 

2) Text-to-Event Matching 
Maps unstructured text to process activities and enables analysis such as: 

  • Which process steps generate the most complaints
  • Where operational issues occur most frequently
     

Industry Use Cases

Detailed Use Cases

Note 
AI-assisted context analyzers only provide analytical decision support. You can then make decisions and trigger actions based on this support. 

 

Prerequisites

Access Requirements 

  • Assigned data management feature sets (see Custom Data Integration Prerequisities) 
  • Editor rights for the objects necessary for data modeling (data pipeline) and data integration (source data set) 
  • Activated AI-assisted context analyzer feature and have sufficient AI units (see Activating Embedded AI Capabilities and Assigning Users) 

Data Requirements 

  • Data view or source data with:  Key field column and Free text column 
  • Each case is linked to one free text entry
  • For cases with multiple texts, only the first text will be analyzed and matched

Pipeline Setup 

  • Case-based data pipeline 
  • Business object and event collector configured and enabled 
  • Extraction, transformation, and load (ETL) has successfully run for the pipeline 
  • Event log data is loaded into analysis configuration 

 

How to Set Up AI-Assisted Context Analyzer 

Step 1: Connect Free Text Data 
Load customer or operational text into SAP Signavio Process Intelligence and ensure it is part of a case-based pipeline. 

 

Step 2: Create Context Analyzer
Go to the Context Analyzers tab in your pipeline and create a new context analyzer.  



Step 3: Choose the Task 
Select the task you want to configure: 

  • Sentiment analysis: Classifies each free text as positive, neutral, or negative 
  • Text-to-event matching: Recommends matches between free text and process events 



Step 4: Select Input Data 

For your input data, choose either from: 

Option A: Existing Data View 
Select your data view and choose the free text and key field columns  

Option B: Existing Source Data
Select your source data and table, then choose the free text and key field columns.  

Review the selected input data in the details card.  

 

Step 5: Configure
Common setting for both tasks: 

  • Anonymization is enabled by default and masks personally identifiable information in the input text (see Data Masking).   

Additional setting for text-to-event matching: 

  • Overlay sentiment label in widget output is available and enabled by default. This displays sentiment scores and color indicators in the Process Discovery widget. 

While both tasks share the same setup flow, their outputs differ in how insights are visualized and analyzed, as shown in the next sections. 

Once the setup is complete, click Create.  



Step 6: Run
To run context analyzers, you can: 

  • Trigger the pipeline run with Run ETL or Run T&L manually 
  • Wait for the next scheduled run 

Track progress in Event Log Load step in the Pipeline Log.

Once the run is complete, you can find the Context Analyzer table with details such as duration, message and execution ID (use Show IDs toggle to display) in Event Log Load step.  

Next, go to the Process Data Model tab to view the output attributes for each context analyzer. 

 

Analyzing Results Using Outputs

Sentiment analysis

  • Sentiment label attribute (case-level)
  • Free text attribute (case-level) 

Text-to-event matching

  • Free text match count metric
  • Free text attribute (event-level) 
  • If sentiment overlay is enabled: 
    • Sentiment score metric (0-100 range) 
    • Sentiment label attribute (event-level)

Step 1: Create a Dashboard
Access your linked analysis configuration by clicking on the card with blue highlight in the Overview tab.  

 

Create a customizable dashboard. 

 

Step 2: Sentiment Analysis Visualization
Create a Charts and Tables widget. 

In widget builder, add the following attributes to dimensions: 

  • Case 
  • Sentiment label (case-level) 
  • Feedback text (case-level)  

 

Step 3: Text-to-Event Visualization
Create a Process Discovery widget.  

 

Select “Text-to-event matches” metric in metric dropdown. You will see the count of free-text records matched to each event. 

 

Select “Text-to-event sentiment score” in metric dropdown. You will see the sentiment-colored overlay on each event along with its score.  

  • A positive sentiment is >70 and has a green overlay 
  • A neutral sentiment is in the range of 50-70 and has an orange overlay
  • A negative sentiment is <50 and has a red overlay  

These ranges and color overlays can be customized (see Progression in Metric Settings). 

 

Once widgets are configured, make sure to save your dashboard to keep your progress.  
 

With AI-assisted context analyzer, unstructured text becomes part of your process analysis. 

By combining sentiment analysis and text-to-event matching, you can enrich your event log with additional attributes and metrics derived from business text. These insights can be analyzed in dashboards to better understand how feedback and operational notes relate to process behavior. 

Once configured, the analyzer runs as part of your data pipeline, enabling continuous analysis without additional manual effort. 

Related Information 

  • AI-Assisted Context Analyzer User Guide 
  • Working with Context Analyzers
  • Step-By-Step Video Tutorial 
  • AI Capabilities Available in SAP Signavio 
  • AI Units 
  • Responsible AI 
  • Data Processing and AI

entry.title: Financial cost with negative sign
entry.content: We have Global hierarchy "MGP1" where for financial cost GL is mapped as "917000". Being a cost, it shows negative sign as per reporting. Cost should always be debit. Run app "MARKET SEGMENT ACTUALS" in S4P. Inputs- LEDGER-0C, GL HEIRARCHY- MGP1 . click on GO. Expand hierarchy. Negative sign for Financial cost will reflect. We wish to make it postive somehow. when passing GL to ACDOCA table we found one billing accounting document where for 0C the GL is getting credited and credit is always negative. This can be resolved with VKOA setting by interchanging GL . But our solution designer dont want to change the VKOA setting so is there any other solution where we can turn the cost to postive sign instead of negative?

entry.title: Re: ABAP development tools for Visual Studio Code: Your Questions Answered
entry.content:
As much as I like the this VSCode initiative, I'm really disappointed about question 2:

"

Will ADT for Visual Studio Code replace ADT for Eclipse?

No. ADT for Visual Studio Code is an additional option, not a replacement.

"

That means that we will have 3 dev tools and (currently) at least 2 need to be used already in parallel:

a) SAP GUI (because Eclipse cannot do everything)

b) Eclipse (because SAP GUI cannot do everything, e.g. CDS, and VSCode also cannot do many things yet)

c) VSCode (and if you now introduce some functionality which only works there, it's really mandatory to work with 3 tools... that would be crazy)

Why can't you enable full feature parity EVERYWHERE and leave the choice to the developers? Already going in with the target of NOT implementing everything is already admitting failure.

I like that this direction keeps productivity high while still staying close to the ABAP ecosystem and enterprise governance needs. The combination of AI-assisted coding and a cleaner developer experience should be very valuable for teams looking to work faster without losing control.

entry.title: Inconsistent Curriculum Assignment Dates Across LMS Views
entry.content:
Hello SAP SuccessFactors Learning Community,

We recently identified an inconsistency in how Curriculum Assignment Dates are displayed across different areas of SAP SuccessFactors Learning.

For the same curriculum assignment, the displayed assignment date may differ between:

  • Admin > Curricula tab
  • Admin > Assigned Items tab
  • Learner view / Learning Plan
  • Reports

SAP confirmed this is currently “expected behavior” related to UTC storage and time zone conversion logic (KBA 3609787). However, from a business and user perspective, this creates confusion because administrators and learners may see different assignment dates for the exact same assignment event.

To address this, I submitted an SAP Customer Influence request asking SAP to provide a more consistent assignment date display approach across LMS.

If your organization has experienced similar confusion or reporting inconsistencies, please consider reviewing and voting for the enhancement request below:

https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/idea/375389

Thank you for your support and feedback.

Serguei,

entry.title: Big News: What’s New in the SAP for Me Services & Support Dashboard (June 3 Release)
entry.content:
🚀Big News: What’s New in the SAP for Me Services & Support Dashboard (June 3 Release)

We’re excited to share a major milestone in the evolution of the SAP for Me Services & Support Dashboard! Following the SAP Sapphire launch, the June 3rd update brings a wave of enhancements designed to simplify navigation, accelerate access to insights, and deliver a more intelligent, streamlined user experience.

Let’s dive into what’s new—and why it matters.

✨ A Smarter Experience with Joule

One of the most exciting changes is the continued rollout of Joule in SAP for Me.

  • The traditional knowledge search bar evolves into an AI-powered intent-driven experience
  • The central search transforms into a magnifying glass icon, encouraging users to engage with Joule
  • Functional search remains available, but AI becomes the primary entry point

Current Knowledge Search Bar

New Joule Bar

👉 This marks a clear shift toward a more intuitive, conversational way to find answers and get things done.

🔄 A Simplified Services & Support Engagements Experience

We’ve reimagined how Services & Support engagements are organized to make them clearer and easier to navigate.

Key highlights:

  • “Pre-Packaged Services” moved from Support Engagements to the Services tab
  • A simplified Support Engagements tab with a Service & Support Entitlements card
  • New Quick Access card:
    • SAP Enterprise Support Academy
    • Sub-Processor
    • Sub-Processor Insights
    • Request Extended Maintenance
    • Customer Center of Expertise (Customer COE)
    • Support Offerings

Updated Services Tab

Updated Support Engagements Tab

👉The result: faster access to the right services, with less clutter and clearer entry points.

🧭 Tab Structure Improvements for a Cleaner Journey

We’ve streamlined the dashboard experience by reorganizing and removing unnecessary elements.

What’s changed:

  • ✔️“More Resources” tab removed entirely
    - Product Availability Matrix → moved to Portfolio & Products
    - Security & Trust Service Center → moved to Finance & Legal

  • ✔️“Diagnostics, Reporting & Analytics” tab repositioned and refined
    - Renamed to “Diagnostics”
    - Focus is now early risk detection with SAP EarlyWatch Alert

  • ✔️“ALM” renamed to “Application Management” with a focus on SAP Cloud ALM
    - All cards consolidated into a central Quick Access card

Removed More Resources Tab


Updated Application Management Tab

📊Early Risk Detection with SAP EarlyWatch Alert

The Diagnostics experience gets a major boost:

  • Dedicated  system health visibility Integration card
  • Quick access to main capabilities (Solution Finder, Reports, Dashboard, Status) and forecast applications
  • One-click subscription for notifications.

New Diagnostics Tab

👉 This helps customers shift toward a more continuous and forward oriented way of managing system risks.

🧹General UX Improvements You’ll Notice Immediately

Beyond the big structural changes, we’ve made several smaller—but impactful—improvements:

  • ❌“Get Assistance” removed from all pages (now only in left-hand navigation)
  • ❌Regulatory Change Manager removed from the dashboard (relocated to Finance & Legal)
  • ✔️Cleaner left-hand navigation experience
  • ✔️Reduced duplication and clearer page purpose across the dashboard

🎯Why This Matters

It’s all about focus, simplicity, and intelligence:

  • 🔍  Less searching, more finding with Joule in SAP for Me
  • 🧭Less navigating, more doing
  • 📊 Better insights, right where you need them
  • ⚡ Faster access to your most important tools and services

🌟What’s Next?

These updates are part of a broader Services & Support Dashboard evolution (Phase 2) - with continued enhancements already planned.

We’re excited to keep building a smarter, more personalized SAP for Me experience together.

💬Other blogs you might find interesting:

  • 🎉Celebrating the Successful Launch of Joule in S... - SAP Community
  • Refining Diagnostics in SAP for Me Services & Support Dashboard

entry.title: Query on report Asset History Report (S_ALR_87011990)
entry.content:
Hi SAP Team,

 

We are currently transferring assets within the same company code using transaction code ABUMN. As a part of the business requirement, we need to change the old cost center to new cost center for the US region. Here business is following bonus depreciation and we need a clarity while doing the asset transfer how the depreciation values to be posted to the new asset.

 

After completing the transfer and posting depreciation, we reviewed the Asset History Report (S_ALR_87011990) for depreciation areas 10, 11, 12, and 13 with the report date of 31.05.2026. We observed the following:

 

  • Success case -For the another deactivated asset L1026787 (Company Code 1000), the report shows a net book value of zero.
  • Failure case - For the deactivated asset C43V3QW3 (Company Code 1000), the report continues to display a current net book value

 

Can you please check and let us know the reason why there is a net book value is showing in the original asset (C43V3QW3/0) even after transferring to the sub asset.</

entry.title: Re: EVENT_PARAMETER cuts long strings at 255 characters
entry.content: The max size of a event parameter is related to the database *server* page size. For a 4k page size, the limit is approximately 4000 bytes. Options for dealing with values that are larger than the server page size might be to break up the parameter values into chunks or perhaps staging the value in a table which is queried in the event passing the primary key of for the values row as a parameter to the event. A unique identifier might be a good option for the key.

entry.title: SAP Incentive Management: Keeping track of Payment Attributes
entry.content:
Hi,

I've been an admin for SAP Incentive Management in my company for a while now. There is a situation that I would like to discuss with this Community, that is the tracking of Payment Attributes, specifically when a recalculation of the period is required.

Let me explain; we use Generic Attributes and Numbers to store accounting values (i.e. various VAT attributes, Supplier ID, GL Account, Cost Center, etc..). After Finalize we extract Deposits and Payments through the RestAPI to generate an Invoice (self-billing) for the upstream system, I've seen a few ERP solutions by now, they all require the same; Payment becomes the Invoice Header and Deposits are used for the Invoice Lines.

Some of the attributes required for the Invoice Header are taken from the Deposits using an aggregated view; attributes like Supplier ID, Name, Start/End/Invoice Date, Legal Entity are the always same for the entire Payment but we can only store values in Generic Attributes in the Deposit.

Whenever we find ourselves in a situation where we really need to rerun a Comp & Pay Pipeline for a Finalized Period, it's all hands on deck, this is an absolute nightmare! Effectively, it's not allowed.

This is what we have to do:

(1) Secure a copy of Deposits for the target Period
(2) Rerun Comp&Pay
(3) Secure a copy of new Deposits
(4) Reset Deposits to original values (Value, GA's, GN's, GB's, GD's)
(5) Recalculate Payment and validate NO BALANCE
(6) Create manual Deposits from delta's between files 1 and 3 in open period

If you made it this far, thank you for reading along. I can almost hear you thinking "Keep Posted Results" (KPR), you are not wrong. I am a strong believer of "make the system work for you" rather then "you work for the system", that's clearly not the case here. As you can see, I mostly work with external partners but this is really no different from integrating with payroll.

My goal is to allow rerun on a Finalized Period and use the KPR data to:

(A) Be able to recreate the original Invoice (i.e. Payment + Deposits)
(B) Be able to create an invoice based on the Applied Balance (Delta between original and new Deposits)

These tables allows me to 'recreate the original Invoice' (A) but I have yet to find a solution to 'create an invoice based on the Applied Balance' (B). I'm just going to ignore releasing Held Deposits for now, to keep me from going insane.

  • CS_PAYMENT
  • CS_BALANCE
  • CS_APPLDEPOSITPAYMENTTRACE
  • CS_BALANCEPAYMENTTRACE
  • CS_APPLDEPOSITBALANCETRACE
  • CS_APPLIEDDEPOSIT
  • CS_KPRDEPOSITAPPLDEPOSITTRACE
  • CS_KPRDEPOSIT

My initial idea was to calculate some kind of delta between the original and new Deposits. I think it would probably be easier to 'simulate' (or in fact automate with a procedure / stagehook) a full reversal of the original Deposits, since the new Deposits would be calculated anyway. This would be easier to account and when aggregated leads to the same delta.

https://chatgpt.com/share/6a2066b2-fe48-8326-a522-1fcc34b914df 

I would love to hear how you (other Comp Admins) deal with these situations. And please, share this post with anyone who you think might be interested.

p.s. I found this thread: Solved: SAP Commissions: Generic Attributes for Payments - SAP Community, which does seem interesting. I doubt it solves anything, you're just moving the Attributes to a different table, one that is hidden from the UI. There are also a number of Feature Requests regarding this topic, none of which seem to be implemented any time soon.

entry.title: Re: the way i create a issue about supplier in running a...
entry.content:
Good day @KTAWA 

Thank you for your question.

 A Supplier capacity or “supplier cannot deliver enough quantity” message will not appear just because you created BOM, routing, purchase info record and work center. These objects do not define a hard supplier delivery capacity.

For supplier selection, you need to maintain Source Lists and if you want multiple suppliers to share the requirement, then maintain Quota Arrangements. Source list will allow you control valid sources for a material and plant, while quota arrangements distribute requirements between available sources.

If your gaol is to show a shortage, create demand that is higher than stock and receipts, run MRP, and check the result in Monitor Material Coverage or Manage Material Coverage. Those apps will help you analyse and resolve material coverage issues.

Here is what I recommend:

  1. Check the product master MRP settings.
  2. Maintain purchase info record for the supplier and material.
  3. Maintain source list that will control valid suppliers.                                                                                  
  4. Maintain quota arrangement if you must split demand between suppliers.       
  5. Create a demand higher than your available supply and run MRP.
  6. Check the shortage in Monitor Material Coverage.                                                            
  7. If your requirement is true supplier capacity confirmation, then you should use supplier confirmations or supplier collaboration because standard procurement does not automatically know the supplier’s physical production or delivery capacity.

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/49fb9b57aba49f2de10000000a44147b.html?locale=en-US 

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/a565c3dddcf943c69dc8d43ac2016e4c.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/2bba750d1e124e1ea2a039bb1cd9b6c5/31c16f543e0de830e10000000a44538d.html?locale=en-US                    

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: PartnerEdge Build: SAP Transportation Management (TM)...
entry.content: SAP TM is embedded in SAP S/4HANA and available in on‑premise, S/4HANA Cloud private edition, and with selected scope in S/4HANA Cloud public edition. If you build your integration using SAP‑released APIs/CDS and a clean‑core, side‑by‑side approach (e.g., on SAP BTP), cloud customers can consume it as well. On‑premise‑style modifications or unreleased objects won’t run in S/4HANA Cloud public edition. For private edition, you have more flexibility, but API‑first keeps your solution portable across editions.

entry.title: Re: Logs or change history of planned orders
entry.content:
Good day @Cosmin05 

Thank you for your question.

If you need a full audit trail like, Planned order, Field Changed, Old value, New value, Changes by and Changed on, then this is normally not available from PLAF alone. In Public Cloud, you cannot solve this by reading internal tables or adding classic table logging. If the standard app and released CDS views do not expose the required history, then it is intended or you can view it as a product gap which you cand consider placing an enhancement request with SAP.

That means a full change history for planned orders is not available from table PLAF alone. PLAF reflects current order header data and can provide change related information such as timestamps, but it does not provide a complete historical log with values mentioned above. See KBA 1803240 on the existence of planned order last change timestamp field.

For the standard process, I suggest you use Manage Planned Orders to check the current planned order  and

use Application Jobs or MRP jobs log to identify whether an MRP run likely changed the planned order.

So, my recommended steps are the following:

  1. Check the planned order in Manage Planned Orders.
  2. Check the MRP run in Application Jobs.
  3. Compare the planned order change date/time with the MRP job execution time.
  4. Use Manage Material coverage to analyse the planning result.
  5. If you must avoid MRP changes, review planning time fence and firming logic.
  6. You may have to raise an improvement request with SAP if a full audit trail is mandatory.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/2bba750d1e124e1ea2a039bb1cd9b6c5/e45ca7eed9f44f828768e720e8d6f7ad.html?locale=en-US

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: How to Implement SAP Business One projects successfully
entry.content:
Great breakdown on the implementation steps. Having run 20+ SMB go-lives in the Hyderabad market, I’d add one critical nuance: the go-live week is where Hyderabad SMBs lose the most money, and it’s almost never a technical issue — it’s a data readiness problem.

Three failure patterns I see repeatedly:

1. Master data validation is skipped under time pressure.

Teams migrate from Tally/legacy spreadsheets and the item master is “good enough” on cutover night. Three weeks later, inventory reports are useless because unit of measure conversions weren’t standardized. Without clean master data, you have zero real-time visibility into what’s actually happening in your warehouse.

The fix: run a parallel inventory count in the old system AND SAP B1 for 2 weeks before go-live. Any variance >2% stops go-live until resolved. This sounds painful, but it’s the single biggest predictor of whether your go-live produces reliable numbers from Day 1.

2. User training is treated as a checkbox.

SMB owners assume the consultant will “handle it.” What actually happens: 3 PowerPoint slides, a rushed demo, and a team that can log in but can’t process a goods receipt. The result: workarounds that corrupt data, followed by 6 months of cleanup.

Better model: identify 2–3 power users per department in week 1. Train them deeply. They become your go-live support army. The goal is not “everyone knows SAP” — it’s “every department has someone who can troubleshoot in real time.”

3. No cutover rollback plan.

If something breaks on go-live night, what’s the decision tree? Who decides to rollback vs. push through? Almost no SMB implementation documents this. The result: 48 hours of chaos before the inevitable rollback — which costs more than if you’d planned for it.

The go-live checklist that actually works:

  • Parallel run confirmation — both systems running simultaneously for at least 5 business days, with real-time variance tracking
  • Master data freeze — no item master, vendor, or customer changes 48 hours before cutover
  • Rollback decision criteria — documented: who decides, under what conditions, and what’s the revert process
  • Go-live support team — internal champion + consultant available for 72 hours straight
  • First-week close check — finance runs a full month-end close in SAP B1 within 5 business days of go-live
  • Batch cost reconciliation — for pharma/manufacturing: verify every component batch traces correctly through the production order to the FG batch. Gaps here become audit findings later.

The single best investment: A formal 3-day parallel run before go-live where the team processes every real transaction in BOTH systems simultaneously. Painful? Yes. Expensive? Yes. Cheaper than data recovery after a bad cutover? Far less.

For pharma and manufacturing companies in Hyderabad (especially in the Genome Valley and Patancheru clusters), the batch cost traceability piece is non-negotiable. If you’re operating under USFDA, WHO-GMP, or EU GMP compliance, your genealogy chain from raw material batches → production order → FG batch has to be complete before your first regulatory audit. Companies that skip this during implementation spend ₹15–40 lakh on remediation later.

We’ve been building AI tooling (TekAI) specifically to give finance teams real-time visibility into batch cost gaps, cashflow forecasts, and inventory KPIs during and after go-live — the kind of operational intelligence that makes the difference between a go-live that “works” and one that actually improves how the business runs from Day 1.

Happy to go deeper on any of these points. What specific module are you struggling with?

— Venkata Siva Reddy Polu, TEKROI
SAP B1 implementation for Hyderabad pharma and manufacturing SMBs

entry.title: Re: Advance Shipment S/4HANA Service Public Cloud
entry.content:
Good day @maurice_broich 

Thank you for your question.

For Public Cloud, the closest standard process is Service Order with Advance Shipment of Spare Parts. You can use this when spare parts need to be shipped in advance, for example:

  • To the customer site before the technician arrives,
  • To the technician before service execution,
  • Or to support field service execution where parts must be available before the work is performed.

However, you must validate the process in your tenant using the available service order item categories and delivery integration. Public Cloud does not allow the same level of IMG based tailoring as Private Cloud, so you must follow the released scope exact configuration steps and logistics behaviour.

In your system, I suggest you check the activated scope and configuration to confirm whether the relevant process is active.

Using Manage Service Orders, create one, enter the sold to party and service object, add a service item if you require, add a spare part item, check whether the spare part item supports advance shipment or delivery relevant processing and save your order. 

Also using Manage Service Orders, open the spare part item, check the item category, plant and storage, quantity and requested date. Check whether logistics follow up or delivery processing is available, then save your order.

Then check outbound delivery and validate the business process.   

See the following Blog and refences for more insights: Serial number management using Cloud Warehouse Management in S/4HANA Cloud

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/adbae5bcd5994f159bf2847a11397b61/8f62c457ace846a2b00df99dd731e6b9.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/adbae5bcd5994f159bf2847a11397b61/0f0d2f82558349b1b4b5ab9465275222.html?locale=en-US

https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-q-a/s-4-public-cloud-service-ship-spare-part-for-a-service-order/qaq-p/14042872

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris
